title: Rock On!

jokes.text: What are we gonna watch, a silent movie from your childhood?
jokes.setup: Johnny announces they're watching an instructional video, implying it's old.
jokes.punchline: Roland's dig implies the video is so old it predates sound — a jab at Johnny's age.
jokes.explanation: A competent opening jab from Roland that sets up his buffoonish but quick-witted character. Not groundbreaking but lands cleanly with good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So what are you watching?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh, just a silent film.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh nice, nice. So you're watching something from your childhood?

jokes.text: it's clear from the stain on your top that things have gotten just a little lax around here
jokes.setup: Johnny is asserting authority by showing a workplace etiquette video.
jokes.punchline: His evidence of the problem is a visible stain on Roland's shirt — an understatement of how bad things are.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's deadpan delivery of a very specific, visual insult is characteristic of his politely exasperated persona. The 'stain' detail makes it concrete and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We need to make a workplace etiquette video for the business.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, great idea! I'm in.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Actually, Roland, you're exactly why we need this video.

jokes.text: I made this at Rose Video a while back, but uh, I think you'll find it holds up pretty well.
jokes.setup: Johnny introduces an instructional video meant to educate his employees.
jokes.punchline: The self-congratulation is immediately undercut by what we're about to see — an obviously dated production.
jokes.explanation: The confidence with which Johnny says 'holds up pretty well' is a setup for the dated content to follow. Rewatch bonus because you know the video is going to be ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: This is very professional. Very well done. Although, I gotta say, some of the techniques they're using here, my wife and I, we've been experimenting with those same angles and positions at home.

jokes.text: Oh wow, this looks like the videos Joce and I used to make.
jokes.setup: Johnny's 'professional' workplace video plays — clearly low-budget and dated.
jokes.punchline: Roland equates it to his own amateur home videos with Jocelyn, implying it's no better than a home movie.
jokes.explanation: A quick, efficient burn that deflates Johnny's pride in the video. Roland doing this accidentally makes it funnier — he's not even trying to be mean.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That behavior is wholly in-app-ro-po

jokes.text: That kind of physical touch is inapprop-ri-rate.
jokes.setup: The instructional video is demonstrating workplace etiquette — a man grabs a woman's arm saying 'let me help you with that.'
jokes.punchline: The actor on the video mispronounces 'inappropriate' as 'inapprop-ri-rate' — a malapropism that undercuts the authority of the instructional content.
jokes.explanation: The malapropism is perfectly placed — an instructional video about professionalism being delivered incompetently. The word 'inapprop-ri-rate' is specifically funny and quotable. High economy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Can we watch that part again?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, that was good. Let's watch it again.

jokes.text: Let's rewind, and try again. And now they're gonna come and do it again!
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to rewind the video to show the interaction again.
jokes.punchline: Roland is both excited and groaning — treating the cringeworthy video like entertainment, with gleeful anticipation of seeing the awkward moment repeated.
jokes.explanation: Roland treating the training video as theater — excited to see the awkward bit again — is character-consistent and funny. Simple but efficient.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Do you have that soap opera compilation? You know, the one with all the close-ups of people's faces when they find out their twin brother's evil?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: We have several compilations like that.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: No, no. This one specifically has the scenes where they find out right before commercial breaks, and there's always that one actor who overacts the gasp.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm going to be honest with you. That describes literally every soap opera ever made.

jokes.text: Is this what you were looking for? A double-cassette of the 'Best Body Swaps of Sunrise Bay', Season 21?
jokes.setup: In the workplace etiquette video, an employee asks another if he needs help finding something.
jokes.punchline: The item 'retrieved' is a comically specific double-cassette of a soap opera's 'best body swaps' — a bizarre artifact that exposes the video was made at a video rental store, and that the etiquette scenario is completely absurd.
jokes.explanation: The specificity is the joke — 'Best Body Swaps of Sunrise Bay, Season 21' is hilarious precisely because it's so absurd and so particular. It also winks at the Rose family's video store background. High originality because the comedic detail is genuinely inventive.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: This is incredible. This is like, peak television.

jokes.text: I value you as a fellow employee. Perhaps you'd like to meet outside of work for a dinner at my place?
jokes.setup: The instructional video is supposed to teach appropriate workplace behavior.
jokes.punchline: The actor demonstrates 'appropriate' behavior by immediately asking his coworker to dinner at his place — exactly the kind of thing the video should be warning against.
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic inversion — the etiquette video models bad etiquette. The formal phrasing ('I value you as a fellow employee') followed by the invitation makes it land well.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: In this scenario, I am demonstrating what NOT to do in the workplace.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Hello, Gerald. I couldn't help but notice you have very nice hands.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Would you like to come back to my office and discuss... the Johnson account?

jokes.text: Fast forward! — Oh! — Jesus.
jokes.setup: The video is playing something more risqué than expected — the workplace dinner invitation has apparently escalated.
jokes.punchline: Johnny desperately fast-forwards while Roland reacts with visceral shock. The reaction of both men implies something truly inappropriate is on screen.
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is entirely in what we DON'T see. The economy of the moment — Fast forward! / Oh! / Jesus. — is excellent. Three words doing heavy lifting.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, no, no, no. We're not watching this.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Why not? I think Moira's doing great!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: She is not doing great, Roland. She is doing the opposite of great.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: If I could skip ahead, I would skip ahead.

jokes.text: Well, it's somewhat dated, but uh... I think the principles of etiquette are still applicable today. Hm. Pretty timeless. Yeah.
jokes.setup: After the shocking video content, Johnny tries to salvage the training session.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's desperate rationalization ('pretty timeless, yeah') and Roland's hollow agreement signal both men know this was a disaster. The understatement is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's embarrassed retreat into 'pretty timeless' is very in-character — he tries to maintain dignity even when the situation is mortifying. The moment lands through performance implied by the text.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I'm so excited about the Poison concert!

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I just hope I don't need to call Poison Control... I mean, the actual Poison Control hotline, not the band!

jokes.text: Uh oh, ladies! Somebody better call Poison Control, because tonight's gonna be... ♪ Nothin' but a good time! ♪
jokes.setup: The women are talking about the Poison concert. Jocelyn bursts in excitedly.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn makes a Poison/Poison Control pun while singing a Poison lyric — a gloriously enthusiastic double-duty joke that perfectly encapsulates her dorky, endearing character.
jokes.explanation: This is a standout moment. The pun works on two levels (the band name / the emergency line), and Jocelyn immediately follows it with an actual Poison lyric — escalating her own bit with perfect comedic timing. It's deeply character-specific: only Jocelyn would prepare a pun this enthusiastically for a Poison concert. The structure is nearly perfect for what it is.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm not gonna be the one to... oh, okay.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn launches into her enthusiastic Poison announcement before anyone can warn her the concert is canceled.
jokes.punchline: Moira's resigned 'oh, okay' as she watches the situation unfold — realizing she missed her window — is a perfectly timed reaction beat.
jokes.explanation: Moira's helpless resignation as Jocelyn steamrolls is great economy — three words, maximum character expression. Works because of what's unsaid.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, apparently Poison wanted to do a joint tour with The Wiggles, and nobody could agree on setlist.

jokes.text: Because when Rikki Rockett makes direct eye contact with you during 'Talk Dirty to Me', you are gonna want to talk dirty back!
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is mid-enthusiasm about the Poison concert, not yet knowing it's canceled.
jokes.punchline: The specificity of Rikki Rockett (the drummer — not even the lead singer) and the fantasy of making eye contact with him during a specific song is absurdly detailed and endearing.
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn choosing Rikki Rockett specifically (the drummer, famously not the frontman) shows the writers' commitment to specificity. The escalating fantasy ('you're gonna want to talk dirty back!') is delivered with full conviction. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I was really looking forward to that concert, but it got cancelled.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I even prepared by pumping before the trip so I wouldn't have to worry about it while I was away.

jokes.text: I didn't pump for four hours straight to get on a chartered bus to go to the end of the street and back!
jokes.setup: The concert is canceled and the group is figuring out what to do instead.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn reveals the hidden sacrifice — she pumped breast milk for four hours for this night out — making her disappointment suddenly much more specific and poignant, and funnier.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The detail 'four hours straight' of pumping transforms what could be generic disappointment into something hyper-specific and real. It's both funny and sympathetic — Jocelyn is a new mother giving herself one night of freedom and this is her sacrifice. The escalation from pumping to chartered bus to 'end of the street and back' builds perfectly. Highly quotable and deeply character-integrated.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Does the cafe have a smoke machine like at a rock concert?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: No, but if you wait long enough, something will catch fire in the kitchen and we'll get that smoky atmosphere naturally.

jokes.text: No, but I can ask George to burn something on the grill.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn asks if the cafe has a smoke machine. Twyla offers the cafe as an alternative.
jokes.punchline: Twyla's literal, pragmatic solution — just burn food — is delivered deadpan and is perfectly in character for her. The gap between what Jocelyn wants (rock concert atmosphere) and what Twyla can offer is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Twyla's earnest literalism is perfectly deployed here. The idea that burning food could substitute for a concert smoke machine is absurd, but her sincerity makes it charming. Clean economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've gambled with some very high-profile people. I once played blackjack with Mariah Carey in Monaco. I lost seventeen thousand dollars to Celine Dion at baccarat in Macau. And I taught David Copperfield how to play poker. Well, I say 'taught' — he made all my chips disappear.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My secret? Tôi không bao giờ thua. That's Vietnamese for 'I never lose.'

jokes.text: If I'm holding the truth stick, I was never planning to attend the concert anyway.
jokes.setup: The group is upset about the canceled concert.
jokes.punchline: Moira casually reveals she never wanted to go in the first place — using the absurd phrase 'holding the truth stick' as if it were a normal idiom.
jokes.explanation: 'Holding the truth stick' is a perfect Moira-ism — theatrical, slightly nonsensical, completely confident. The admission itself is also funny because it reframes the entire situation. Strong character integration and quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just realized I can't go to the casino.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Because I have a gambling problem.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Since when?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Since I was like, seven.

jokes.text: When Condi Rice, and Sharon Stone and I, used to make our annual casino sojourn to Ho Chi Minh City, well, let's just say, toi khong bao gio thua.
jokes.setup: The group is wondering if Moira is a gambler. She responds by establishing her gambling credentials.
jokes.punchline: Moira casually name-drops Condoleezza Rice and Sharon Stone as gambling companions in Vietnam, then delivers the punchline in Vietnamese — 'I never lose' — as if this is completely normal.
jokes.explanation: This is peak Moira. The combination of Condi Rice + Sharon Stone as gambling companions is absurdist in exactly the right way for her character — these two women have absolutely nothing in common except Moira's social mythology. Delivering the punchline in Vietnamese is the chef's kiss — it requires the audience to be told later (or look it up) what she said, and the confidence of the delivery is perfect. This is among the episode's best moments.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what? I wasn't listening.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You weren't listening? We're in the middle of discussing our biggest client.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, my apologies. I was thinking about something.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: About what?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: About us. About whether you still find me attractive.

jokes.text: And this is the only casino I haven't been banned from as an unaccompanied minor.
jokes.setup: Moira is establishing why she's okay going to this particular casino.
jokes.punchline: The implication is that she has been banned from other casinos as an unaccompanied minor — meaning she was gambling unsupervised as a child at multiple establishments.
jokes.explanation: Extraordinary economy — this one line reveals a chaotic, unsupervised childhood, multiple casino banishments, and Moira's complete lack of self-awareness about how wild this sounds. The 'as an unaccompanied minor' qualifier is doing massive comedic work. Highly quotable. One of the episode's standout jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know what it's like to be married to someone completely self-absorbed and difficult.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, David is literally the worst.

jokes.text: So what exactly am I paying you for, then? / I'm your brand consultant, David. I'm consulting with you on which template to use.
jokes.setup: Alexis explains that a website builder does most of the work automatically.
jokes.punchline: When David questions her value, Alexis redefines her role as 'template consultant' — a perfectly circular logic that deflates the concept of her expertise.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's non-answer is a great character beat — she's completely unashamed of her minimal value-add. The circular logic ('I'm consulting on the template') is funny in its shamelessness.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Do you have a pen and paper?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, are you writing a suicide note? Because that's the only reason anyone asks for those two things together.

jokes.text: Sorry, I'm just distracted, there's a guy over there currently flirting with my boyfriend. / Poor thing. / I know. / No, I meant you, David.
jokes.setup: David points out a guy flirting with Patrick, expecting sympathy from Alexis.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's 'poor thing' appears to be sympathy for David — until she clarifies she means David is the poor thing for being jealous, not Patrick for being hit on.
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection — we assume Alexis's sympathy is directed at David's situation (the flirtation), then the clarification redirects it at David himself (his jealousy). The beats are tightly structured and character-specific. Alexis gently mocking David's insecurity while he tries to play it cool is a sibling dynamic that works well.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Ken? Just... Ken? Not Kenneth, not Kendrick, not even a hyphenated situation? Just Ken?

jokes.text: Um... score, Patrick! Look at you, just drowning in other people's phone numbers. Who was that hunk with the teeny-weeny little polo?
jokes.setup: A customer has just given Patrick his phone number.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's enthusiastic reaction ('drowning in other people's phone numbers') and her description of the guy ('hunk with the teeny-weeny little polo') is simultaneously complimentary to Ken and oblivious to David's feelings.
jokes.explanation: 'Teeny-weeny little polo' is a great physical comedy descriptor — you can picture exactly the shirt. Alexis's enthusiasm is deliberately tone-deaf to David's situation, which is her whole character.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ken, you must have been doing this for like, what, forty years?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, that is an absolutely inappropriate thing to say to someone. I mean, really, the audacity. Ken is a consummate professional and I won't have you sitting here, making these kinds of ageist remarks in front of him. It's offensive, it's rude, and frankly, it's beneath you.

jokes.text: Just when I thought it was impossible to find a thirty-something named Ken!
jokes.setup: Patrick reveals the man's name is Ken.
jokes.punchline: Alexis treats the name 'Ken' as comically dated for a person's actual age — implying Ken is a name for an older generation or a Barbie doll.
jokes.explanation: The joke about Ken being an unlikely name for a younger person has a Barbie/Ken undercurrent that also plays into Patrick's situation. Good economy and Alexis-specific delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know, if I had just been a little bit nicer to that customer, they probably would have bought everything in the store. Actually, no. If I had been there, they would have bought everything in the store AND asked for my autograph.

jokes.text: Oh, I think he looked younger than 30. / Get fucked Alexis!
jokes.setup: Alexis implies Ken is too old to be named Ken.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's mild correction and David's explosive 'get fucked Alexis' — an unusually profane outburst from David — is the punchline. The aggression reveals his jealousy perfectly.
jokes.explanation: David's 'get fucked Alexis' is a great character moment — his composure cracks just enough to reveal genuine jealousy. The contrast between Patrick's mild 'I think he looked younger' and David's sharp retort is perfectly structured.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: She gave me her number!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, she was giving you her number so you could call her about the condo listing.

jokes.text: What exactly did Ken do with my pen? Did he write down a banana bread recipe? Or was he looking for directions to some place?
jokes.setup: Patrick has received Ken's phone number. David pretends he hasn't understood what happened.
jokes.punchline: David's increasingly absurd 'innocent' suggestions for what Ken wrote — banana bread recipe, directions — perform his denial of the obvious. The absurdity escalates beautifully.
jokes.explanation: The two alternative suggestions ('banana bread recipe,' 'directions') are perfectly chosen — mundane enough to be plausible, specific enough to be funny. David's performance of ignorance is deeply character-specific: he's too proud to admit jealousy directly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: She was married with kids.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: And he like... gave it to you in front of David, so clearly no threat there.
jokes.setup: Patrick has received a phone number from Ken while David was right there.
jokes.punchline: Alexis reads Ken's boldness not as a threat but as proof David is harmless — hilariously misreading (or deliberately poking) the situation.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's interpretation is either oblivious or deliberate provocation — both are funny. The implication that David cuts an unthreatening figure is a solid sibling jab.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Stevie! What are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh my God! Jesus! You can't just walk in here!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I didn't know you were— what is all this?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's not what it looks like.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It looks like you're taking pictures of yourself.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay, it's exactly what it looks like, but you cannot tell anyone about this. I'm serious, Johnny.

jokes.text: Hey Alexis, find a stick...
jokes.setup: Alexis has said Ken clearly sees David as no threat.
jokes.punchline: David's trailing 'find a stick...' (implying 'and beat it' or similar) — left incomplete, which makes it funnier. The implied insult is sharper for being unfinished.
jokes.explanation: The incomplete insult is a good technique — leaving the punchline to the audience's imagination. Economy is high precisely because it doesn't finish the thought.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I was taking photos for medical reasons.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: My doctor said I need to document things for my... condition.

jokes.text: You know, just last week, a woman came in and gave me her number. / Do you mean the woman who wanted to know when the soap would be back in stock? Who came in here with her husband and kids?
jokes.setup: Patrick is trying to feel good about himself getting a number from Ken by offering his own anecdote of a woman giving him her number.
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately identifies the woman as a married mother asking about soap restocking — completely deflating Patrick's claim to romantic success.
jokes.explanation: Strong structure — Patrick's brag is systematically dismantled by Alexis's perfect recall of exactly who the woman was. The accumulation of details ('husband and kids') is the comedic escalation. Alexis's matter-of-fact delivery is essential.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh, I see. So you're saying that you were... busy with work?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, that's—that's perfectly understandable. I mean, we all have our... our priorities. And yours just happen to involve a computer.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know what? That's actually very forward-thinking of you. Very... modern. I respect that. I respect technology.

jokes.text: Johnny walks in on Stevie taking a topless photo for a long-distance partner, saying 'I said just a minute!' while Johnny backs away saying 'Yeah, yeah.'
jokes.setup: Johnny comes to find Stevie at the motel to discuss the latticework, calling out before entering.
jokes.punchline: He walks in anyway and catches her mid-intimate-photo. The cringe of the situation — manager walking in on employee — is maximized by both parties' reactions.
jokes.explanation: A classic comedic premise executed with character-specific awkwardness. The setup (latticework discussion!) makes the interruption more absurd. Rewatch bonus for context — you re-understand everything once you know what's happening.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I wish workplace training covered this.

jokes.text: Taking a personal photo... For my doctor. Right, well, it was, it was a medical uh, photo, and, and uh... and you what? You text or email this right back to your doctor, I mean, what a-what an age we live in!
jokes.setup: Stevie hastily explains she was taking a photo 'for her doctor.'
jokes.punchline: Johnny desperately accepts the excuse and even celebrates the wonders of technology ('what an age we live in!') as a face-saving device for both of them.
jokes.explanation: 'What an age we live in!' is perfect Johnny — desperately polite, trying to normalize the situation through enthusiasm for technology. His complicity in the lie is both kind and hilarious. The awkward stammering ('and, and uh...') is also doing heavy work. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, you know, Stevie, there are certain moments in life that are meant to be private. And I think it's important to respect that privacy, even if it means being a little... unconventional about it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What I'm trying to say is, don't be embarrassed. We all have our... needs. The key is to handle them with dignity and discretion.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Just make sure you lock the door. And perhaps invest in a white noise machine.

jokes.text: I wish we had gotten to the second tape in the workplace etiquette series, that had a whole chunk on Polaroids.
jokes.setup: Stevie has been caught taking intimate photos. Johnny tries to defuse the situation.
jokes.punchline: Johnny callbacks the earlier training video plot by implying the etiquette video covered Polaroids — a joke that both references the scene we just watched and implies the older technology.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback — ties the episode's A and B plots together economically. Johnny using the failed training video as a reference point for the current situation is both character-perfect (he's still trying to maintain professional dignity) and structurally satisfying. The Polaroid detail is funny because it implies the video's age and the kind of incidents it anticipated.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I wore this dress because I thought it would be appropriate for the casino, but now I'm wondering if I look like our old babysitter who used to wear things that were way too revealing.

jokes.text: All I saw was a business partner communicating with a friend, that's all. Although you've just explained that it was a more intimate relationship with this uh, chap. But uh, congratulations, by the way. If-if uh, you know.
jokes.setup: Stevie admits she's seeing someone long-distance after being caught taking the photo.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's increasingly tangled effort to be supportive while maintaining professional decorum — ending with the dangling 'if-if uh, you know' — is perfectly awkward.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's halting congratulations — where he can't quite commit to what he's congratulating — is pure character comedy. The 'if-if uh, you know' trailing off is a great non-verbal (textual) beat.

jokes.text: And if you feel the need to finish that communication by the way, with your friend, just uh, maybe try and find a room with a locked door?
jokes.setup: Johnny is wrapping up the awkward conversation and telling Stevie to take a walk.
jokes.punchline: His final piece of advice — 'find a room with a locked door' — is both practically sound and so delicately phrased that it circles back from being helpful to being funny.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's parting advice is perfect — simultaneously fatherly, professional, and deeply uncomfortable. The delicacy of 'finish that communication... with your friend' is outstanding Johnny-speak. Practical advice that happens to be extremely funny.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have my casino essentials: a change of clothes, a sensible shoe, caffeine pills, and a cover story that I got these from Condoleezza Rice.

jokes.text: I waited 35 minutes! / Oh, don't start.
jokes.setup: Johnny comes out of the motel having resolved the situation with Stevie.
jokes.punchline: Roland has been waiting outside the whole time, oblivious to everything, and his complaint is the perfectly mundane counterpoint to everything we just witnessed.
jokes.explanation: Good scene button — Johnny's exhausted 'oh, don't start' after everything he's just been through is a perfect tag. Roland's obliviousness is his defining character trait deployed efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I could bring some brownies I made. They're very popular at the community center.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, they have a very special ingredient that really brings people together.

jokes.text: You know what? I was going to change and then I thought, no, if my babysitter can show up wearing a crop top the size of a bib, I can wear this to a casino!
jokes.setup: The women are heading to the casino. Jocelyn arrives in presumably casual/inappropriate attire.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's logic: if the babysitter gets away with a 'crop top the size of a bib,' her own outfit is fair game. The specificity of 'the size of a bib' is the detail that lands.
jokes.explanation: 'Crop top the size of a bib' is a great, specific image. Jocelyn's competitive logic — if she gets away with it, so can I — is relatable and endearing. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I made some brownies for the casino trip. They're regular brownies, but I also made some special brownies if anyone wants them.

jokes.text: Yes, why not throw caution and the dress code to the wind? I'm sure the maître d' at Bucky's will make an exception!
jokes.setup: Jocelyn declares she's going to the casino in her current outfit.
jokes.punchline: Moira's faux-encouragement ('throw caution and the dress code to the wind') is a backhanded observation, undercut further by mentioning Bucky's Fried Chicken — implying any dress code concerns are overblown given the venue.
jokes.explanation: The Bucky's Fried Chicken callback lands well — Moira is both encouraging and gently mocking. Her elevated language applied to a fried chicken establishment is a class-gap joke that Schitt's Creek does very well.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I made these brownies, but I assume you won't want any since they have marijuana in them.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh no, I'll have one. Actually, make it two. Three? How many did you make?

jokes.text: If the bus is a-rockin', don't come a-knockin'! Wooo!
jokes.setup: The group boards a charter bus to the casino.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's enthusiasm applies a 'don't come a-knockin' bumper sticker phrase to a group of middle-aged women on their way to a casino — the gap between her excitement and the mundane reality is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A fairly generic enthusiasm moment — the phrase isn't particularly fresh, but Jocelyn's commitment to it and the setting make it land mildly. Competent but not special.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, you should date Ken.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Why would I date Ken?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because if you date him and it works out, great. And if you date him and it doesn't work out, you'll realize what you're missing and come crawling back to me. Either way, I win.

jokes.text: I've come prepared with casino must-haves: oxygen drops, antibacterial wipes, and military-grade caffeine pills. The pills came from Condi, so if anyone asks, you found them on the floor.
jokes.setup: Moira says she came prepared for the casino.
jokes.punchline: Her 'must-haves' are increasingly absurd — oxygen drops, antibacterial wipes — culminating in 'military-grade caffeine pills from Condi' with the instruction to deny having them. The Condi Rice callback plus the illegal-drug parody is a great escalation.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Condi Rice makes this joke land even harder on rewatch. The list structure builds beautifully — oxygen drops and wipes feel like Moira hypochondria, then the military-grade caffeine pills from Condi with plausible deniability is a perfect escalation. 'If anyone asks, you found them on the floor' is a hilarious instruction that implies these pills could get someone in trouble. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What happened earlier?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I slept with Roland.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What?!

jokes.text: Who wants some zhampagne?
jokes.setup: Moira has provided military-grade stimulants; Jocelyn has provided magic brownies.
jokes.punchline: Twyla's contribution is champagne — but pronounced 'zhampagne' — the anti-climax of a perfectly normal thing delivered with equal enthusiasm, as if it belongs in the same category as the drugs.
jokes.explanation: One word — 'zhampagne' — does a lot. The pronunciation choice and the anti-climax of offering champagne after magic brownies and military pills is a good comedic beat. High economy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I thought of something to say about this situation.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm gonna write some really inappropriate poetry about it.

jokes.text: Magic brownies! / THC and carbohydrates. / Oh Moira, we don't expect you to have one. / One? At least one.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn produces 'magic brownies.' Moira is expected to be too proper to partake.
jokes.punchline: Moira corrects the assumption with 'one? At least one' — revealing she's far more enthusiastic about getting high than anyone expected.
jokes.explanation: The misdirection — we expect Moira to be squeamish — followed by her enthusiastic correction is a great subversion of type. 'One? At least one.' is economical and perfectly delivered. High character integration because it deepens Moira's backstory of excess.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh my God!

jokes.text: David asking what happened to the phone number while Patrick mentions it 'might still be in his pocket' — and then acting surprised at the implication.
jokes.setup: David is pretending not to care about Ken's number but angles for information.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's 'might still be in my pocket' suggests he kept it — and then he notes David's reaction to that.
jokes.explanation: A character beat more than a joke, but the dynamic of David feigning indifference while clearly monitoring the situation is a sustained comedy of manners. Competent execution.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, it appears that Roland has discovered texting.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: How delightful for him.

jokes.text: Best case scenario, you realize how good you have it with me. Worst case scenario, you realize how good you have it with me.
jokes.setup: David is explaining to Patrick why he should go on a date with Ken.
jokes.punchline: The 'best/worst case scenario' structure promises contrasting outcomes, then delivers the same answer twice — a confident joke that simultaneously reveals David's insecurity and his genuine confidence in their relationship.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. The best/worst case scenario structure is a comedy classic, but the subversion — giving the SAME answer — is executed with precision. It works on multiple levels: it's a confident declaration of David's worth AND a subtle admission that the only acceptable outcome is Patrick coming back. The structure is nearly perfect, the economy is exceptional, and it's deeply character-integrated. This is the kind of line that gets quoted and remembered.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, Patrick's having dinner with Ken? Ew, why would he do that?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because Ken is his boss.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, but like, Ken is so... I don't know, he's like a thumb in a blazer.

jokes.text: What happened? / Mr. Rose saw me topless.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Stevie have just agreed to put an incident behind them. Roland asks what happened.
jokes.punchline: Stevie delivers the blunt summary — 'Mr. Rose saw me topless' — with zero softening, in complete contrast to the delicate way Johnny has been handling it.
jokes.explanation: Five words. The contrast between Johnny's elaborate circumlocutions to avoid saying exactly this, and Stevie's flat, immediate declaration, is structurally perfect. The economy here is exceptional — Roland gets his answer, Johnny is horrified, and the audience gets the punchline they've been waiting for. Stevie's deadpan is essential to why this works.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I realized that if I date someone famous, they're too busy to cheat on me. Like, Brad Pitt doesn't have time to see someone else — he's got a charity to run, or whatever.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's... that's not how celebrity relationships work, Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, it should be. It's a very solid strategy.

jokes.text: No! No! I... I didn't completely see anything, but I got the gist of what you were doing.
jokes.setup: Roland reacts to finding out Johnny saw Stevie topless.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's horrified denial — followed by the admission that he 'got the gist' — is a perfect, self-undermining correction. He's trying to exonerate himself but only makes it worse.
jokes.explanation: 'I got the gist of what you were doing' is a perfectly understated way of saying he understood the situation without confirming exactly what he saw. The qualifier 'I didn't completely see anything' followed by that admission makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Ken? Please. He's not a threat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, he's literally gorgeous. He has those perfect teeth, and that hair, and those arms...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, well, he works at a plant nursery.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So?

jokes.text: Oh Johnny, I don't think the woman in that video would approve. / The woman on the video is Moira, and no, she would not approve! I would not approve!
jokes.setup: Roland references the workplace etiquette video in response to Johnny seeing Stevie topless.
jokes.punchline: Johnny clarifies that the woman in the video is his wife Moira — and immediately adopts Moira's (imagined) disapproval as his own, deflecting and panicking at the same time.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that connects both plotlines of the episode. Roland's observation that the video-woman wouldn't approve is clever, Johnny's horrified revelation that it's Moira deepens the joke. The exclamation 'I would not approve!' — Johnny speaking for Moira in the third person — is a great character beat showing his fear of her judgment.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So wait, you're saying that in an open relationship, you can just see other people?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, Alexis, that's the entire point of an open relationship.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, that's like... that's like having Josh Groban's relationship manual.

jokes.text: Well, I did just think of one thing. / Please don't! / It's more of a limerick. / All right, enough, Roland.
jokes.setup: Johnny tells Roland that in this situation, 'less is more.'
jokes.punchline: Roland ignores the guidance and attempts to deliver a limerick about the situation. The joke is in Johnny's preemptive 'please don't' before Roland even finishes his sentence.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the structure: Johnny says 'less is more,' Roland immediately prepares to do more. 'It's more of a limerick' is a funny specification. Johnny's exhausted pre-emptive 'please don't' is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Aah! Stevie! Your friend just responded! / Oh my God, gimme that! / Oh my... uh, I'll be taking the rest of the day off. / Okay. Yeah... I'd say her friend responded.
jokes.setup: Stevie's phone buzzes. Roland has seen the notification/image.
jokes.punchline: Stevie grabs the phone, sees what was sent back, and immediately announces she's taking the day off. Johnny's closing 'I'd say her friend responded' is the button.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from the embarrassing incident to the returned photo is a satisfying comedic payoff. Johnny's button line — 'I'd say her friend responded' — is a perfect understatement. The economy of Stevie just saying 'I'll be taking the rest of the day off' lands very well.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, I went to see the Doodlebops because they were performing their hit song 'The Wiggle Dance (But Make It Fashion).'

jokes.text: He has plans with someone else. / Who? / Okay, can you utilize like, a little bit more of the English language?
jokes.setup: Alexis finds out David isn't going to Patrick's tonight.
jokes.punchline: Alexis can only manage 'Who?' — and David's exasperated demand for more complete sentences from her is a sibling dynamic that lands with recognition.
jokes.explanation: David's demand for 'more of the English language' is a funny, characteristic exasperation. The economy is good — Alexis says almost nothing, David says volumes.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I rocked with the Rolling Stones.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You did not rock with the Rolling Stones.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was there, David. I was at their after-party in the '70s.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mother, you were not at a Rolling Stones after-party.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I would have been, if Charlie Watts hadn't been such a party pooper.

jokes.text: Every time I did that with a boyfriend, I'd send them off with someone who like, wasn't a threat. Like, Pippa Middleton, or Rihanna.
jokes.setup: Alexis is describing her strategy of sending boyfriends to spend time with 'non-threatening' people.
jokes.punchline: Her examples of 'non-threats' are Pippa Middleton and Rihanna — two objectively very attractive, famous women — which completely undermines the concept of a 'non-threat' and reveals Alexis's warped self-perception.
jokes.explanation: The choice of Pippa Middleton and Rihanna as 'non-threats' is the entire joke and it's excellent. Alexis is so accustomed to moving in rarefied circles that globally famous, gorgeous women don't register as competition. This is peak Alexis — the name-dropping serves the comedy perfectly rather than being gratuitous. High originality and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, Jocelyn, I've made peace with aging. You see, wrinkles are just a map of all the places your face has been.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And at my age, I've been to many, many places. Usually in search of a good skincare regimen, but places nonetheless.

jokes.text: So what are the GRs? / The what? / The ground rules. What ground rules did you lay down before you sent him off with that hottie-boom-bottie?
jokes.setup: David sent Patrick off to dinner with Ken without establishing parameters.
jokes.punchline: 'GRs' (ground rules) as shorthand, plus 'hottie-boom-bottie' as Alexis's descriptor for Ken — both are pure Alexis-speak that lands through sheer character specificity.
jokes.explanation: 'Hottie-boom-bottie' is quintessential Alexis — a made-up compound word that only she would use, deployed with complete confidence. 'GRs' suggests she has done this enough times to develop shorthand. Very high character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, darling, you are 19 on the inside. And you know what that means? You should be gambling.

jokes.text: Josh Groban has a thick, leather-bound binder full of them.
jokes.setup: Alexis is explaining that David needs ground rules for Patrick's night out.
jokes.punchline: Alexis reveals that Josh Groban has a leather-bound binder of ground rules — implying she has personal knowledge of this, and that her relationship with Josh Groban involved extensive rule-making.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The specificity of 'Josh Groban' (not a generic celebrity but an oddly specific choice), paired with 'thick, leather-bound binder,' creates a perfect absurdist image. It implies an entire relationship backstory with no further explanation needed. The confidence with which Alexis mentions this as if it's normal information is essential. Extremely quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, Emir seems like a very capable, intelligent young man. Solid business acumen. I respect that.

jokes.text: I have never been in this position before, usually I'm the one finding out I'm in an open relationship after the fact.
jokes.setup: David is panicking that he may have made a mistake sending Patrick off with Ken.
jokes.punchline: David's admission that his previous relationships involved discovering open arrangements retroactively — rather than agreeing to them — is a dark, perfectly character-specific joke about his romantic history.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant dark joke that reveals David's entire romantic past in one sentence. The idea that he's always been on the receiving end of someone else's unilateral 'open relationship' decision is simultaneously sad and funny. It's deeply specific to David's character and history. High craft.

jokes.text: It's either black or red, even or odd. And you never know where you're gonna land.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn appears to be at the roulette wheel, stoned.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's philosophical musing on roulette is clearly also about life — delivered with the deep sincerity of someone who is stoned — making it both funny and unexpectedly poignant.
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's stoned philosophizing at the roulette wheel is a sweet character moment. The joke is mild but lands through her sincerity — the comedy is in context rather than content.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So David came home, and he was like, really upset. And I was like, 'David, what's wrong?' And he was like, 'I can't talk about it right now.' So I said, 'Okay, well, we're having dinner at 6:30.' And he said, 'Alexis, that's in twenty minutes.' And I was like, 'I know.'

jokes.text: If you'd've told me 20 years ago I'd be standing here with an empty chip bucket, stoned out of my mind at a Doodlebops concert, I would've lit a menthol, and had a laugh.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn and Moira are at the casino.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's hypothetical — imagining her younger self's reaction to her current situation — is packed with specific, funny details: empty chip bucket, stoned, Doodlebops (a children's show), and the younger self smoking a menthol and laughing. Each detail adds to the comedy.
jokes.explanation: The accumulation of specific, sad-funny details is excellent: 'empty chip bucket' (she's already lost), 'stoned out of my mind' (from Jocelyn, of all people), 'Doodlebops concert' (the thing that replaced Poison), 'lit a menthol' (period-specific, class-specific), 'had a laugh.' The Doodlebops callback pays off the double-booking joke from earlier beautifully. Character-integrated and genuinely charming.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I couldn't do it. I looked at his shoes and they were square-toed. Square-toed! I can't be with someone who wears square-toed shoes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's fair.

jokes.text: You went to that show? / I stepped in briefly, Roland Jr.'s a huge fan of 'Wobbly Whoopsy.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn mentions being at a Doodlebops concert.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Jocelyn 'stepped in briefly' — at a children's concert — and that the specific character Roland Jr. loves is named 'Wobbly Whoopsy' is a perfect absurdist detail.
jokes.explanation: 'Wobbly Whoopsy' is a great invented character name — specific enough to feel real, absurd enough to be funny. The detail that Jocelyn 'stepped in briefly' implies she was drifting between a children's show and a casino, stoned.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I did something really brave today. I cut my hair.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Do you think it makes me look like a lesbian?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Not that there's anything wrong with that. My therapist says I need to work on my internalized homophobia.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: But also, do I look like one?

jokes.text: Well, of course you did, as did anyone with brain cells to kill. I'm thinking of a particular night in Monaco with Maggie Trudeau and the Rolling Stones. / You rocked with the Stones? / All except Charlie, but every party has a pooper.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn says she 'used to rock out.' Moira agrees that this is normal.
jokes.punchline: Moira's story of 'rocking with the Rolling Stones in Monaco with Maggie Trudeau' is a perfect Moira anecdote — glamorous, name-dropping, specific. The punchline is that she excludes Charlie Watts, the drummer, with 'every party has a pooper,' implying Charlie Watts was a buzzkill.
jokes.explanation: The joke requires specific knowledge — Charlie Watts was the Rolling Stones' drummer, famously reserved and sober — which makes it play as an insider joke for rock fans. 'Every party has a pooper' is a perfect phrase for Moira to deploy. The Maggie Trudeau detail (Pierre Trudeau's wife, who famously partied with the Stones) shows the writers' attention to real rock history. Highly quotable and deeply character-specific. One of the episode's best Moira moments.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Do you think my haircut looks okay?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: No, not really. You look like you got your hair caught in a screen door.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Every Rose Has Its Thorn... and so does your head.

jokes.text: Oh Jocelyn, you'll soon learn that we aging mortals are blessed with weakening eyes and memories so we don't have to really see ourselves.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn laments that she feels 19 on the inside but catches herself in the mirror.
jokes.punchline: Moira reframes aging decline — weakening eyes and memory — as a mercy rather than a curse. The dark philosophical observation is delivered as wisdom.
jokes.explanation: A genuinely clever dark observation that works both as comedy and as something approaching wisdom. Moira reframing deterioration as 'blessings' is a perfect expression of her theatrical, self-protective worldview. Quotable and character-specific.

jokes.text: Oh Jocelyn, you'll soon learn that we aging mortals are blessed with weakening eyes and memories so we don't have to really see ourselves.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn laments that she feels 19 on the inside but sees something different in the mirror.
jokes.punchline: Moira reframes aging eyesight and memory loss as a mercy — a cosmic gift that protects us from confronting our own deterioration. Delivered with genuine comfort.
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed joke that also functions as genuine wisdom. The idea that biological deterioration of senses is actually a kind gift — 'so we don't have to really see ourselves' — is both dark and consoling. Moira delivering it with sincerity makes it land better than if it were ironic. Highly quotable and the kind of line that sticks with viewers.

jokes.text: I'm glad things are working out with you and your gentleman friend. And I'm just piecing things together here.
jokes.setup: Johnny is putting on his 'friend cap' to speak to Stevie about her personal life.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm just piecing things together here' is an excellent understatement — he's using what he saw (and didn't see) to construct who Emir is to Stevie.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's polite euphemism for 'I saw things I can't unsee' is classic Johnny. The comedy is in his sustained effort to be appropriate and kind despite having stumbled into something deeply inappropriate.

jokes.text: Remember Emir? The guy who came to review the motel? The one who asked you to lunch? Yes, that would be him. Oh! Well, he seems like a nice guy.
jokes.setup: Johnny said he's staying out of it and they never have to discuss it again.
jokes.punchline: Johnny immediately asks about Emir — demonstrating he cannot help himself. His 'yes, that would be him / oh well he seems like a nice guy' is a perfectly awkward reversal.
jokes.explanation: Johnny says 'we never have to talk about this again' and then immediately starts talking about it again. This is a great structural irony — his curiosity overriding his propriety. The detail of him knowing exactly who Emir is ('the one who asked you to lunch') implies he's been paying more attention than he let on.

jokes.text: And I'm not saying that just because of the photo. / Oh my God, Mr. Rose! / Okay, rewind, please.
jokes.setup: Johnny is praising Emir for liking Stevie a lot.
jokes.punchline: Johnny says he's not basing this on 'the photo' — reminding both himself and Stevie of the incident — and then immediately backtracks with 'rewind, please.' The callback lands perfectly.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's unintentional re-opening of the wound ('not just because of the photo') followed by his own horrified 'rewind, please' — using the exact verb from the training video scene — is a beautifully economical callback that works on two levels. High craft.

jokes.text: And I'm not saying that just because of the photo. / Oh my God, Mr. Rose! / Okay, rewind, please. / See you tomorrow! / Yeah, okay, see you tomorrow, Stevie.
jokes.setup: Johnny says he can tell Emir likes Stevie — then adds 'I'm not saying that just because of the photo.'
jokes.punchline: Having navigated the whole awkward situation, Johnny accidentally invokes the photo again, mortifying both of them. 'Okay, rewind, please' and Stevie's immediate departure is perfect.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the photo lands perfectly here. Johnny has been trying so hard to be appropriate, then accidentally torpedoes the nice moment by mentioning the very thing he was trying to forget. 'Okay, rewind, please' is a great callback to the actual video-rewinding from the opening. Stevie's immediate exit is the right button.

jokes.text: No! Who knew everything would contain chicken? / Oh, that dessert menu was especially disappointing. 'Black forest tenders.'
jokes.setup: The women return from Bucky's Fried Chicken House.
jokes.punchline: Moira's horror at chicken-based everything, and Twyla's specific callback of 'Black forest tenders' — a chicken interpretation of Black Forest cake — is a perfect absurdist detail about a terrible restaurant.
jokes.explanation: 'Black Forest tenders' is a great invented dish — the specificity of combining Black Forest cake (a German chocolate dessert) with chicken tenders is absurd in exactly the right way. Moira's 'who knew everything would contain chicken' is a good setup, but the tenders are the real punchline.

jokes.text: Tah-dah! Oh, Jocelyn, is that you? / Yes! I took your advice, Moira.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has been missing. She reappears.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn has dramatically changed her hair — clearly a big, impulsive haircut — and Moira's 'is that you?' implies it's genuinely hard to recognize her.
jokes.explanation: The reveal of Jocelyn's dramatic new haircut is a visual gag that depends entirely on the audience seeing it. Moira's horrified 'is that you?' is a polite version of 'oh no.' Solid setup/reveal structure.

jokes.text: That I can still rock on! / Your chips bought this!
jokes.setup: Moira asked what advice led to Jocelyn's dramatic haircut.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn reveals she gambled away her chips on a haircut, interpreting Moira's advice to 'rock on' literally and financially. The callbacks to both the 'rock on' advice and the casino chips pay off simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: The payoff that Jocelyn spent her casino winnings on an impulsive haircut is a great structural callback. 'Your chips bought this!' is both accusatory and delighted — only Jocelyn could be simultaneously blaming Moira and proud of herself.

jokes.text: Yes, Jocelyn, what advice did I impart that translated into this daring new coiffure?
jokes.setup: Moira is confronted with the apparent consequences of her encouragement.
jokes.punchline: Moira's phrasing — 'what advice did I impart that translated into this daring new coiffure?' — is performing concern and fascination while clearly having no memory of saying anything that extreme. 'Daring new coiffure' is doing a lot of euphemistic work.
jokes.explanation: 'Daring new coiffure' is a perfect Moira euphemism — maximum vocabulary, minimum commitment to an opinion. She's simultaneously alarmed and maintaining aesthetic diplomacy.

jokes.text: And you certainly risked it all, didn't you? Yeah!
jokes.setup: Jocelyn explains she bet her chips on 19 (Moira's advice) and won, and used the winnings to get the haircut
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'you certainly risked it all' is ostensibly about gambling but clearly refers to the haircut — the double meaning works on both levels
jokes.explanation: A clean double-meaning joke. 'Risked it all' works for both the roulette bet and the haircut decision. Well-constructed.

jokes.text: And you certainly risked it all, didn't you?
jokes.setup: Jocelyn reveals her dramatic haircut and says she 'risked it all' as Moira advised.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'you certainly risked it all' is a perfectly calibrated understatement — agreeing with Jocelyn while clearly being horrified by the result.
jokes.explanation: Moira's 'you certainly risked it all' does double duty — technically agreeing with Jocelyn while implying the risk did not pay off. The tone distinction between what she says and what she means is pure Moira.

jokes.text: Wow, Jocelyn. It must feel so light and breezy.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has gotten an extremely short haircut. Everyone is struggling with their reaction.
jokes.punchline: Twyla, unable to say anything genuinely positive, lands on 'it must feel so light and breezy' — the most innocent, non-committal response possible. It's technically a compliment about the practical aspect of short hair, while avoiding any aesthetic judgment.
jokes.explanation: Twyla's 'light and breezy' is a masterclass in polite evasion — a sentence that says absolutely nothing about the haircut's appearance while seeming supportive. The word choice ('breezy') is specific and funny. Very high economy: she says everything and nothing at once. This is peak Twyla — earnestly trying to be kind while being unable to actually compliment what she sees.

jokes.text: Hey Ted, I'll be there soon, but I just can't leave David alone right now. Yeah, no, apparently they left for dinner at seven. Radio silence. Nothing, not a word.
jokes.setup: Alexis is checking in with Ted while allegedly keeping David company during Patrick's dinner with Ken.
jokes.punchline: Alexis is clearly tracking the Ken-Patrick situation more anxiously than David is (or more than she lets on) — 'radio silence, nothing, not a word' reveals she's been monitoring, and her reason for staying with David is as much self-interested drama as genuine support.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's dramatic 'radio silence, nothing, not a word' signals her own investment in the drama. Good character beat — she's as hungry for the drama as she is concerned about David.

jokes.text: Oh my God, imagine? Muah! Muah!
jokes.setup: Alexis is speculating about what Patrick and Ken might be doing.
jokes.punchline: Alexis mimes kisses ('muah! muah!') while imagining Patrick and Ken together — delighting in the drama she's helped create, which is both funny and Alexis-specific.
jokes.explanation: A visual/physical beat — Alexis miming kisses — that's funny primarily in performance. Less effective in transcript form. Solid Alexis characterization but not among the episode's best.

jokes.text: This is not how I wanted to spend my night. / Me neither.
jokes.setup: Both David and Alexis are stuck waiting for news from Patrick.
jokes.punchline: David's 'Me neither' — delivered with the weariness of someone who created this situation — is the perfect response. He's the architect of his own misery.
jokes.explanation: Economy is the star — 'Me neither' does a lot of work. David's exhausted self-awareness that he caused all of this is delivered with perfect deadpan. The joke is in the resignation.

jokes.text: David, I know what you're thinking, they're probably back at his house, listening to Frank Ocean, laughing, and re-hydrating.
jokes.setup: David is spiraling about where Patrick and Ken might be.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's extremely specific scenario — Frank Ocean, laughing, 're-hydrating' — implies a post-exercise/post-romantic scenario described in the most pointed possible way.
jokes.explanation: Frank Ocean as the choice of romantic/mood music is a culturally specific choice that's perfect for the character. 'Re-hydrating' as the final, worst implication of what they're doing is a great euphemism — Alexis is almost graphically implying something while maintaining plausible deniability. High character integration.

jokes.text: if a cute guy that walks into your store is enough to unravel your whole relationship, you're better off knowing that now, than in a year from now.
jokes.setup: Alexis is trying to help David contextualize his situation.
jokes.punchline: What sounds like genuine wisdom is also a backhanded way of saying David's relationship might be fragile — Alexis accidentally delivers good relationship advice while also being slightly insulting.
jokes.explanation: Alexis delivering genuine wisdom while still being slightly cutting is a character evolution moment played for mild comedy. The line works both as actual advice and as a comedic jab at David's anxiety.

jokes.text: I couldn't do it. / I don't think I ever said 'date'. / Was it the lack of ground rules?
jokes.setup: Patrick returns early from his dinner with Ken.
jokes.punchline: David's semantic dodge ('I never said date') and Alexis's immediate callback to the 'ground rules' conversation are both good character beats firing simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: David's 'I never said date' is a classic defensive semantic distinction. Alexis immediately cutting in with 'was it the lack of ground rules?' is a great callback that shows she's been tracking this situation closely. Good comedic layering.

jokes.text: Okay, I mean... if that's what you want, then I guess I'm... I'm fine with it, I mean, I'm easy either way. / Oh.
jokes.setup: Patrick says he feels no need to meet other guys. David tries to appear casual about it.
jokes.punchline: David's 'I'm easy either way' — spectacularly unconvincing — and Patrick's flat 'Oh.' are a perfect two-beat exchange. The entire episode's tension deflates into David's transparent relief and Patrick's amused acknowledgment.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is entirely in what David doesn't say. 'I'm easy either way' — from a man who has been visibly spiraling all episode — is the perfect mask-slipping moment. Patrick's 'Oh.' (one word, implying 'sure, Jan') is perfectly calibrated. The contrast between David's performed indifference and his obvious relief is the whole joke.

jokes.text: Also, this is gonna sound totally petty, but something about his shoes just made me feel really weird, 'cause they were like, long and pointy, but then... / Squared off at the toe. / Yes! Yeah. Yeah, yeah, yeah, I know. I know, it's okay.
jokes.setup: Patrick is explaining why he couldn't go through with dinner with Ken.
jokes.punchline: Patrick describes Ken's shoes as 'long and pointy, but then squared off at the toe' — a very specific fashion crime. David completing the description ('squared off at the toe') reveals he already clocked the shoes. The shared aesthetic horror is both funny and a perfect relationship moment.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's most charming and funniest moments. The specificity of 'long and pointy, but then squared off at the toe' is a real, recognizable shoe design failure. But the real joke is that David immediately knows exactly which shoes Patrick means — completing the sentence — revealing that David also noticed and was also disturbed. It's simultaneously funny (shoe snobbery as dealbreaker) and deeply romantic (they share the same hyper-specific aesthetic judgments). Extremely high character integration — this could ONLY be David and Patrick.

jokes.text: Okay, this is a really long hug now. / Just need one more minute, mhmm.
jokes.setup: David and Patrick reconcile with a hug. Alexis is still in the room.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's observation that the hug is 'really long now,' and David's 'just one more minute' (while still in the hug), is a sweet comic beat — David needing the comfort acknowledged, Alexis being the witness.
jokes.explanation: A warm, funny button to the David/Patrick storyline. David's 'just need one more minute' — acknowledging the hug is overstaying its welcome while refusing to end it — is honest and funny. Works because the comedy and the emotion are inseparable here.

jokes.text: Okay, this is a really long hug now. / Just need one more minute, mhmm.
jokes.setup: Patrick and David reunite and share a long emotional hug.
jokes.punchline: Alexis, still in the room, notes the hug has gone on too long, and David's 'Just need one more minute' — muffled, clearly mid-hug — is both emotionally true and funny.
jokes.explanation: 'Just need one more minute, mhmm' is a sweet and funny acknowledgment that David is emotionally processing through the hug. Alexis's practical observation grounds it. Good scene-closing beat.

jokes.text: We all still like it, right? / I think it really brings out my eyes, right, Gals? / Sure. / It's not like we think it's a mistake, or we regret it, right? / Oh, you never regret a good time, Jocelyn. / Thank you, Moira. / You'd tell me the truth, I know. / It's really short.
jokes.setup: The women are on the bus home. Jocelyn is seeking reassurance about her drastic haircut.
jokes.punchline: The escalating reassurances — each more evasive than the last ('sure,' 'you never regret a good time') — end with Twyla's brutally honest 'It's really short.' After all the polite hedging, this simple truth lands like a bomb.
jokes.explanation: The structure is excellent — increasingly desperate reassurance questions from Jocelyn, increasingly evasive non-answers from Moira and Twyla, until Twyla simply says 'it's really short.' The escalation and the final deflation are well-constructed. Moira's 'you never regret a good time' is especially good because it answers the question without answering the question. Twyla's final deadpan is the episode's best button.

jokes.text: ♪ Every rose has its thorn ♪ [played over final scene]
jokes.setup: The episode ends with Jocelyn's bad haircut and the group processing it.
jokes.punchline: Playing a Poison song ('Every Rose Has Its Thorn') over the final shot — after an entire episode about a canceled Poison concert — is a meta callback to the episode's premise. The song's lyric ('every rose has its thorn') also winks at the Rose family.
jokes.explanation: A structural and musical callback that works on two levels: the Poison song finally plays (after they never made it to the concert), and 'Every Rose Has Its Thorn' references the Rose family name. The economy of delivering this as a musical sting rather than dialogue is excellent. Rewatch bonus for appreciating the layering.

tmdb_overview: Moira and the Jazzagals take Jocelyn out for a night at the casino, while Johnny tries to navigate an awkward conversation with Stevie about intimacy in the workplace.
insight: Musical-genre parody carries a 79.5 score on character comedy alone, sans standout zingers.
